areaTag.zh: 家事法 · 财产（临时）· 配偶赡养

one.zh: 财产（临时）——妻子申请配偶赡养与诉讼资金；双方婚内生活奢华，但妻子未能证明其无力充分自养，赡养申请被拒；法院按「一比一」对等方式作出有期限的诉讼资助令。
one.en: Property (interim) — the wife sought spousal maintenance and litigation funding; the parties had an extravagant marital lifestyle but the wife did not establish an incapacity to support herself adequately, so maintenance was refused; a time-limited dollar-for-dollar litigation-funding order was made.

issue.zh: 临时阶段：妻子是否证明其「无力充分自养」以获得配偶赡养（s 72/s 75）？以及在丈夫可经家族取得资金的情况下，是否应作出诉讼资助令（s 114UB）？
issue.en: On an interim basis: has the wife established she is 'unable to support herself adequately' for spousal maintenance (s 72/s 75)? And, given the husband's access to family funds, should a litigation-funding order be made (s 114UB)?

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Interim orders – Where the wife seeks orders for spousal maintenance and litigation funding – Where the parties enjoyed an extravagant lifestyle during the marriage – Where the wife has not established an incapacity to support herself adequately – Where the significant dispute in the substantive proceedings relate to the ownership of companies – Where, on any case, the husband has access to significant funds through the paternal grandparents – Litigation funding order made on a dollar-for-dollar basis for a limited duration.

subseq.zh: 本案为临时裁定；实体财产分割、第三方实体异议（定于 2026-06-15）等留待后续。本资料库未见进一步记录。
subseq.en: Interim ruling; the substantive property case and third-party entity objections (listed 15 Jun 2026) remain. No further record in this library.

hint.en: The whole case as a story

html.zh:
双方均出生于中国。妻 Ms Van（1992 年生）2015 年 1 月移居澳大利亚、现为永久居民；夫 Mr Aixin（1983 年生）1999 年来澳、已入籍。两人约 2016 年结婚，2025 年 4–5 月间分居，育有一子 X（2019 年生），现随母生活、按同意令与父相处。

夫是「Aixin 集团」旗下众多企业的 CEO，2024 财年收入约 55.9 万澳元。妻称夫为至少 64 家公司的董事或股东。夫的说法则是：集团真正的所有人与控制人是祖母（祖父因在中国任上市公司 CEO 不便具名），自己只是代表父母在澳经营、是「外国投资者的挂名人（front）」，并不拥有集团权益——此点妻有争议。妻则任 Aixin 基金会执行董事并领薪，另经营 K Pty Ltd。

本次妻提出三项临时请求：① 命令夫（及集团）作财务披露；② 配偶赡养每月 61,435.55 澳元；③ 诉讼资助（25 万一次性，或按「一比一」对等）。夫反对，并请求委任独立鉴定会计师/估价师审查妻的支出与奢侈品。

CasePocket：McNab 法官的处理是——部分准予披露（涉第三方实体的异议留到 6 月 15 日再审，以保程序公平）；驳回配偶赡养（妻未证明「无力充分自养」）；准予按「一比一」对等的诉讼资助令（有期限，至 2026-09-01 或调解结束）。
html.en:
Both parties were born in China. The wife, Ms Van (b. 1992), moved to Australia in January 2015 and is now a permanent resident; the husband, Mr Aixin (b. 1983), came to Australia in 1999 and is a citizen. They married around 2016 and separated in April–May 2025. They have one child, X (b. 2019), who lives with the wife and spends time with the father under consent orders.

The husband is CEO of the many businesses in the "Aixin Group" and earned about $558,818 in FY2024. The wife says he is a director or shareholder of at least 64 companies. The husband's account is that the true owner and controller of the group is the paternal grandmother (the grandfather, CEO of a listed company in China, did not want to appear as owner), and that he merely represents his parents and is a "front for a foreign investor", owning no interest in the group — which the wife disputes. The wife is an executive director of the Aixin Foundation (and is paid) and also runs K Pty Ltd.

The wife sought three interim orders: (1) financial disclosure by the husband (and the group); (2) spousal maintenance of $61,435.55 per month; and (3) litigation funding ($250,000 lump sum or, alternatively, dollar-for-dollar). The husband opposed and sought single experts to examine the wife's spending and her luxury collection.

CasePocket: McNab J's outcome — partial disclosure (third-party entity objections held over to 15 June for fairness); spousal maintenance refused (no incapacity to self-support shown); and a dollar-for-dollar litigation-funding order made, time-limited to 1 Sep 2026 or the end of mediation.

hint.en: The judge's path, step by step

t.zh: 判决路径 · 案件走向

html.zh:
1
披露：依 s 71B 与规则 r 6.06，各方须充分坦诚披露财务。法院命令夫披露其名下相关实体；但涉第三方实体的异议留到 2026-06-15 再审，以免程序不公。 ›
2
配偶赡养门槛 s 72：唯有当对方「无力充分自养」（因照顾未成年子女／年龄或身心障碍／其他充分理由）时，另一方才负赡养义务。 ›
3
合理标准 ≠ 婚内奢华（Wilson）：对申请人而言「合理」的生活水准，未必等于婚姻存续期间或对方所享的水准。 ›
4
妻所列「需求」——每周食物 1,800、衣物 2,000 澳元——被法院评为「entirely fatuous（全然荒谬）」；申请人须自证其据，法院不会代为臆测一个「合理」数额。
5
妻未证明「无力自养」——33 岁、受过教育、健康、经营金融服务业务、且握有可观资金；「无力」不会被推定（Rowan / Stacy / Parsons）→ 赡养驳回。 ›
6
诉讼资助 s 114UB(2)/(3)：在「公正」时可作费用令；「一比一」对等令属最后手段，常用于财力占优方由亲属/关联方供资之情形（Atkins）。夫可经家族取得资金、妻处弱势 → 准予一比一，至 2026-09-01 或调解结束。 ›
html.en:
1
Disclosure: under s 71B and r 6.06 each party must give full and frank financial disclosure. The court ordered the husband to disclose his own related entities, but held the third-party entity objections over to 15 June 2026 to avoid procedural unfairness. ›
2
Maintenance threshold s 72: one party is liable only where the other is unable to support themselves adequately (caring for a child under 18, age/incapacity, or another adequate reason). ›
3
Reasonable standard ≠ marital extravagance (Wilson): what is "reasonable" for the applicant is not necessarily the standard enjoyed during cohabitation or by the payer. ›
4
The wife's claimed "needs" — $1,800/week on food, $2,000/week on clothing — were "entirely fatuous"; the applicant must prove her case, and the court will not invent some other "reasonable" figure.
5
The wife did not show an inability to self-support — 33, educated, healthy, runs a financial-services business and holds significant funds; inability is not inferred (Rowan / Stacy / Parsons) → maintenance refused. ›
6
Litigation funding s 114UB(2)/(3): a costs order may be made where "just"; a dollar-for-dollar order is a last resort, apt where the stronger party is funded by relatives/associates (Atkins). The husband had family funding and the wife was disadvantaged → dollar-for-dollar ordered, to 1 Sep 2026 or end of mediation. ›

CasePocket：配偶赡养看 s 72（门槛）+ s 75(2)（考量因素）；诉讼资助看 s 114UB；披露看 s 71B + r 6.06。
html.en:

§ r 6.06 ›
CasePocket: Maintenance turns on s 72 (threshold) + s 75(2) (factors); funding on s 114UB; disclosure on s 71B + r 6.06.

html.zh:

hint.en: Why the judge decided this way

html.zh:
1. 披露——准其所需、护其程序。妻有权检验夫「我只是外国投资者挂名人、不拥有该集团」的证据，故法院命令夫披露其名下 C Pty Ltd 等实体文件；但涉及第三方实体的传票异议双方已约定 2026-06-15 开庭，现在就裁会「先发制人」、对异议方不公，故暂不处理。

2. 配偶赡养——先过门槛，再谈数额。s 72 的逻辑是：先确立「需求/无力自养」，才谈赡养。妻每周 1,800 食物、2,000 衣物之主张被法院直斥「fatuous」；其辩称「这是高净值人群的生活成本，不应以普通社会标准衡量」，法院不接受——据 Wilson，合理标准未必等于婚内奢华。法院亦拒绝「替妻另估一个合理数额」："it is incumbent on a party moving for orders to file evidence which might support the orders sought… the applicant must prove her case."

3. 妻并非无力自养。妻 33 岁、受过教育、健康，经营面向「成熟/专业投资者」的金融服务业务；账户有大额进出（数十万澳元的存入、退款、对外支付），且自述曾为投资募资逾 2,000 万。"There is no liability to pay spousal maintenance unless the need of the applicant is first established." 据 Rowan / Stacy / Parsons，「无力」不会被推定 → 赡养驳回。

4. 诉讼资助——准予一比一。s 114UB(3) 任一因素即可启动裁量。法院据 Salvage & Fosse 的法理（防止掌控资产一方独占资源请律师）与 Atkins 的「最后手段」情形——尤其财力占优方由亲属/关联公司供资——认定：夫确经家族取得资金、妻处弱势，故作一比一对等资助令，并限期至 2026-09-01 或调解结束，以控成本与不确定性。
html.en:
1. Disclosure — allow what is needed, protect fairness. The wife is entitled to test the husband's evidence that he is a mere "front for a foreign investor" owning nothing, so the court ordered disclosure of his own entities (the C Pty Ltd group). But the third-party entities' subpoena objections were, by consent, listed for 15 June 2026; deciding now would pre-empt that and be unfair to the objectors, so the court held off.

2. Maintenance — clear the threshold before the number. Section 72's logic: establish need/inability first, then talk quantum. The wife's $1,800/week food and $2,000/week clothing were branded "fatuous"; her argument that "these are the costs of high-wealth living, not to be judged by ordinary standards" was rejected — per Wilson, the reasonable standard is not necessarily marital extravagance. The court also refused to substitute its own "reasonable" figure: "it is incumbent on a party moving for orders to file evidence which might support the orders sought… the applicant must prove her case."

3. The wife is not unable to support herself. She is 33, educated and healthy, and runs a financial-services business for "sophisticated and professional investors"; her accounts showed large movements (hundreds of thousands in deposits, refunds and outgoings), and she had reportedly raised over $20 million for investment. "There is no liability to pay spousal maintenance unless the need of the applicant is first established." Per Rowan / Stacy / Parsons, inability is not inferred → maintenance refused.

4. Litigation funding — dollar-for-dollar granted. Any single s 114UB(3) factor can enliven the discretion. Drawing on Salvage & Fosse (preventing the asset-controlling party from monopolising resources for lawyers) and Atkins (a last-resort order, apt where the stronger party is funded by relatives/associated companies), the court found the husband had family funding and the wife was disadvantaged, and made a dollar-for-dollar order, time-limited to 1 Sep 2026 or the end of mediation to contain cost and uncertainty.

hint.en: What you can cite it for

t.zh: 本案确立什么 · 可援引于
t.en: What this case establishes · cite for

html.zh:
确立/重申：

① 配偶赡养须先确立「无力充分自养」（s 72）——「无力」不会被推定；申请人须以可信证据自证需求，法院不会代为臆测一个合理数额（Rowan / Stacy / Parsons）。

② 合理生活标准 ≠ 婚内奢华（Wilson）——不能以「高净值人群成本」之名要求维持奢侈生活。

③ 诉讼资助（s 114UB）可作「一比一」对等令，尤其当财力占优一方由亲属/关联方供资、而另一方处弱势时（Atkins、Salvage & Fosse、Zschokke）。

④ 程序公平优先：涉第三方实体的披露/传票异议，应待异议方获完整听审后再决，避免「先发制人」。

可援引于：反对「以婚内奢华标准索取临时赡养」；强调赡养门槛与举证责任；以及在一方由家族供资时争取对等诉讼资助。
html.en:
Establishes / reaffirms:

(1) Spousal maintenance requires first establishing an inability to support oneself adequately (s 72) — inability is not inferred; the applicant must prove need with credible evidence, and the court will not invent a reasonable figure (Rowan / Stacy / Parsons).

(2) A reasonable standard ≠ marital extravagance (Wilson) — "high-wealth living costs" cannot be used to demand the continuation of a lavish lifestyle.

(3) Litigation funding (s 114UB) may be ordered dollar-for-dollar, especially where the stronger party is funded by relatives/associates and the other is disadvantaged (Atkins, Salvage & Fosse, Zschokke).

(4) Procedural fairness first: disclosure/subpoena objections involving third-party entities should await a full hearing for the objectors rather than being pre-empted.

Cite it for: resisting interim maintenance pitched at a marital-extravagance standard; the maintenance threshold and onus; and securing dollar-for-dollar funding where one party is bankrolled by family.

html.zh:
spousal maintenance (s 72)配偶赡养——一方在「无力充分自养」时由另一方供养
unable to support adequately无力充分自养（s 72 的门槛）
s 75(2) factors行使赡养/财产裁量时的考量因素清单
litigation funding / dollar-for-dollar诉讼资助 / 一比一（对等）资助令——你花多少、对方付你同额
duty of disclosure (s 71B)充分坦诚披露财务的义务
interim / interlocutory临时（在终局裁判前的过渡性命令）
fatuous荒谬不实（法院对妻所列开支的评价）
offers of settlement (r 4.11)和解要约的交换（限期互发要约/还价）
html.en:
spousal maintenance (s 72)Support of one spouse by the other where they cannot adequately support themselves
unable to support adequatelyThe s 72 threshold for maintenance
s 75(2) factorsThe list of matters considered when exercising the maintenance/property discretion
litigation funding / dollar-for-dollarAn order that the other party pay you the same amount you spend on legal costs
duty of disclosure (s 71B)The duty to give full and frank financial disclosure
interim / interlocutoryA temporary order made before the final hearing
fatuousFoolish/baseless — the court's view of the wife's claimed expenses
offers of settlement (r 4.11)A timed exchange of written offers/counter-offers to settle

desc.zh: 财务事项程序的各方均负有及时、充分、坦诚披露的义务——包括其知悉的信息及其曾经或现在持有/控制的文件。
desc.en: Each party to a financial proceeding has a duty to give full and frank disclosure in a timely manner — of information known to them and documents in or previously in their possession or control.

src.zh: 据 s 71B（及 r 6.06）命令夫披露其名下相关实体文件。
src.en: Under s 71B (and r 6.06) the husband was ordered to disclose his related entities.

desc.zh: 一方对另一方负赡养义务，当且仅当另一方「无力充分自养」（因照顾未满 18 岁子女／因年龄或身心障碍无法适当受雇／或其他充分理由），并须顾及 s 75(2) 各因素。
desc.en: A party is liable to maintain the other if, and only if, the other is 'unable to support themselves adequately' (by caring for a child under 18, by age or incapacity, or for any other adequate reason), having regard to the s 75(2) matters.

act.zh: 72 Right of spouse to maintenance —— (1) A party to a marriage is liable to maintain the other party, to the extent that the first-mentioned party is reasonably able to do so, if, and only if, that other party is unable to support herself or himself adequately whether — (a) by reason of having the care and control of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years; (b) by reason of age or physical or mental incapacity for appropriate gainful employment; or (c) for any other adequate reason; having regard to any relevant matter referred to in subsection 75(2).
act.en: 72 Right of spouse to maintenance — (1) A party to a marriage is liable to maintain the other party, to the extent that the first-mentioned party is reasonably able to do so, if, and only if, that other party is unable to support herself or himself adequately whether — (a) by reason of having the care and control of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years; (b) by reason of age or physical or mental incapacity for appropriate gainful employment; or (c) for any other adequate reason; having regard to any relevant matter referred to in subsection 75(2).

src.zh: 法院认定妻未跨过 s 72 的「无力自养」门槛。
src.en: The court found the wife did not cross the s 72 'unable to support' threshold.

desc.zh: s 75(2) 列出行使赡养裁量时须考量的因素：家暴影响、年龄与健康、收入财产与受雇能力、照顾未成年子女、各自必要承担、拟作的 s 79 财产命令，及公正所需的其他情形。
desc.en: Section 75(2) lists the matters for the maintenance discretion: the effect of family violence, age and health, income/property/resources and capacity for employment, care of a child under 18, necessary commitments, any proposed s 79 property order, and any other matter the justice of the case requires.

src.zh: 法院据 s 75(2) 认定不应为维持奢华生活而作赡养令。
src.en: Under s 75(2) the court declined to order maintenance to sustain a lavish lifestyle.

desc.zh: 一般规则是各自负担费用（(1)）；但在「公正」之情形，法院可作任何认为公正的费用或费用担保命令（(2)），并须顾及 (3) 列各因素（双方财务状况、行为、是否完全败诉、和解要约等）——任一因素即可能具决定性。诉讼资助令（含一比一）即据此作出。
desc.en: The general rule is each party bears their own costs ((1)); but where 'just', the court may make any costs or security-for-costs order it considers just ((2)), having regard to the (3) factors (the parties' finances, conduct, whether wholly unsuccessful, settlement offers, etc.) — any one factor may be determinative. Litigation-funding (including dollar-for-dollar) orders are made under this section.

src.zh: 据 s 114UB(2)/(3) 作出一比一对等诉讼资助令。
src.en: A dollar-for-dollar litigation-funding order was made under s 114UB(2)/(3).

desc.zh: 就财产事项，法院命令双方限期互换书面和解要约（夫 14 日内发要约、妻 28 日内接受或还价），以促成早日解决。
desc.en: The court ordered a timed exchange of written settlement offers on the property issues (husband to offer within 14 days; wife to accept or counter within 28) to encourage early resolution.

src.zh: 法院据 r 4.11 命令交换和解要约。
src.en: The court ordered an exchange of offers under r 4.11.

desc.zh: 细化财务披露范围：收入（含支付/转给他人的）、各类既得或或然的财产权益、由其全部/部分拥有或控制之实体的权益与收入、其他财务资源、相关信托、可能影响请求的财产处分、负债与或然负债等。
desc.en: Specifies the scope of financial disclosure: earnings (including income paid/assigned to others), vested or contingent property interests, interests and income of entities wholly/partly owned or controlled, other financial resources, relevant trusts, dispositions of property that may affect a claim, and liabilities and contingent liabilities.

src.zh: 法院据 s 71B 与 r 6.06 框定夫的披露义务。
src.en: The court framed the husband's disclosure by s 71B and r 6.06.

summary.zh: 对申请人而言「合理」的生活水准，未必等于支付方所享、或婚姻存续期间所享的水准。
summary.en: The standard of living reasonable for the applicant is not necessarily that enjoyed by the payer, or during cohabitation.

prop.zh: 合理赡养标准 ≠ 婚内/对方的奢华水准。
prop.en: A reasonable maintenance standard ≠ the marital/payer's lavish standard.

Wilson.passage: "A standard of living that in all the circumstances is reasonable for the party claiming maintenance is not necessarily the same standard as that enjoyed by the party who is ordered to pay maintenance… nor… necessarily the same standard as that enjoyed during cohabitation."

src.zh: 法院据 Wilson 否定以婚内奢华为据的赡养主张。
src.en: The court used Wilson to reject a maintenance claim pitched at marital extravagance.

note.zh: ✦ 全席判例（未纳入本资料库）。
note.en: ✦ Full Court authority (not in this library).

summary.zh: 除非先确立申请人的需求/无力自养，否则不能作赡养令；「无力」不会被推定。
summary.en: Maintenance cannot be ordered unless the applicant's need/inability to self-support is first established; inability is not inferred.

prop.en: Maintenance requires proof of inability to self-support.

src.zh: 据 Rowan 认定妻未确立需求。
src.en: Per Rowan, the wife had not established need.

note.zh: ✦ 经典判例（未纳入本资料库）。

summary.zh: 与 Rowan 同旨：申请人须先确立无力充分自养，赡养义务方生。
summary.en: To the same effect as Rowan: liability arises only once inability to self-support adequately is established.

prop.zh: 无力自养是赡养的前提。
prop.en: Inability to self-support is a precondition to maintenance.

src.zh: 与 Rowan、Parsons 并引，支持驳回赡养。
src.en: Cited with Rowan and Parsons to refuse maintenance.

note.zh: ✦ 经典判例（未纳入本资料库）。

summary.zh: 近期同庭判例，重申赡养的门槛与举证：须先确立无力自养（[32]–[38]）。
summary.en: A recent same-court decision restating the threshold and onus: inability to self-support must be established first (at [32]–[38]).

prop.zh: 重申赡养门槛与举证责任。
prop.en: Restates the maintenance threshold and onus.

src.zh: 法院引 Parsons [32]–[38] 支持其结论。

note.zh: ✦ 同庭判例（未纳入本资料库）。

summary.zh: Watts 法官指出：一比一对等令是裁量性的「最后手段」；典型情形包括——掌控财源者声称无力、弱势方指不出可用资产，而占优方却能经个人收入/其控制的结构、或亲属/关联方供资来打官司。
summary.en: Watts J: a dollar-for-dollar order is a discretionary order of 'last resort'; examples include where the party controlling the purse-strings pleads impecuniosity while funding their own litigation through income/controlled structures, or via relatives/associates, and the disadvantaged party cannot point to a fund.

prop.zh: 一比一对等令的「最后手段」情形（含亲属供资）。
prop.en: The 'last resort' circumstances for a dollar-for-dollar order (incl. relative funding).

Atkins.passage: "The making of a dollar for dollar order is a discretionary order that is usually made only as an order of last resort… Where a financially advantaged party has relatives or associates who are prepared to fund the litigation against the financially disadvantaged party…"

src.zh: 法院据 Atkins 的情形作一比一资助令。
src.en: The court applied Atkins to make a dollar-for-dollar order.

summary.zh: Campton 法官梳理 s 114UB 诉讼资助：须先满足「公正」方启动裁量（否则维持各自负担之原则）；(3) 各因素中任一即可能具决定性；并汇集 Salvage & Fosse、Zschokke 等原则。
summary.en: Campton J reviewed litigation funding under s 114UB: the court must first be satisfied the order is 'just' (otherwise each party bears their own costs); any one of the (3) factors may be determinative; collecting the principles in Salvage & Fosse and Zschokke.

prop.zh: s 114UB 诉讼资助的框架与原则汇编。
prop.en: A synthesis of the s 114UB litigation-funding framework.

src.zh: 法院据 Naumov (No 2) 的框架行使裁量。
src.en: The court applied the Naumov (No 2) framework.

note.zh: ✦ 同庭判例（未纳入本资料库）。

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

MCNAB J

These reasons concern interim property orders, including spousal maintenance and litigation funding, sought by the applicant wife, Ms Van born in 1992 (“the wife”) and opposed by the respondent husband Mr Aixin born in 1983 (“the husband”).

BACKGROUND

The wife and husband were both born in China.[1] The wife moved to Australian in January 2015 and is now a permanent resident.[2] The husband relocated to Australia in 1999 and is an Australian citizen.[3] The husband says the parties commenced cohabitation in 2015, were married in mid-2016 and separated on 28 April 2025.[4] The wife gives different evidence where she says that the parties commenced a long-distance relationship until she emigrated to Australia in January 2015, were married in early 2016 and separated on a final basis on 11 May 2025.[5] The basis for the conflicting evidence is unclear, but, in any case, for the purposes of the present applications it is of little consequence.
The parties have one child to the marriage, being X born in 2019 (“the child”). The child lives with the wife and spends time with the husband pursuant to interim orders made by consent on 7 April 2026. Parenting proceedings remain ongoing otherwise.
The husband is employed as the CEO of various businesses (“[Aixin Group]”). In the 2024 financial year, the husband earned about $558,818.[6] The wife’s evidence is that until July 2025, the husband’s salary and distributions from the Aixin Group were deposited into a joint bank account and were then applied to meet living expenses but that since July 2025 they were redirected into an account that she has no visibility over.[7]
From the wife’s understanding, the husband is a director or shareholder in at least 64 companies which form the Aixin Group. These are separated into sub-groups, the J Property Group, the Aixin Capital Group and the Aixin Family Group. Further, there is the Aixin Foundation which is a philanthropic venture.[8]
The essence of the husband’s evidence is that the paternal grandmother is the ultimate owner and controller of Aixin Group (noting that the paternal grandfather did not want to appear as the owner of any private businesses in Australia given his capacity as the CEO of a publicly listed company in China) and he assist his parents.[9] In this respect, he says that:[10]
(a) the paternal grandparents were to make all decisions in relation to the investment strategy in Australia;

(b) the husband was to act as the paternal grandparents’ representative in respect of their business ventures in Australia and the paternal grandparents would invest their own funds into property investments in Australia;

(c) to facilitate the paternal grandparents’ businesses, the husband would, on their behalf, establish corporate entities in Australia; and

(d) if, in the paternal grandparents’ opinion, the business was successful and remained so for 12 months, they would, at their discretion, provide the husband with a reward in the form of a 10% interest.

It is therefore the husband’s contention that he does not own, control and/or have an interest in the Aixin Group.[11] This is disputed by the wife.
The wife is currently employed as an executive director of the Aixin Foundation,[12] and receives a wage notwithstanding that the husband says she has undertaken little work in that capacity since separation.[13] Otherwise, the wife operates a business, K Pty Ltd. She says that it does not make a profit and she does not draw a wage.[14] The wife’s income in the financial years ending 30 June 2022 until 2024 was about $124,000.[15] This comprised of her salary from her employment with Aixin Foundation and income from J Property Fund. In the 2025 financial year, the husband notes that the wife earned $119,898.[16]

EVIDENCE

By way of her Outline of Case filed 27 March 2026, the wife relies upon:
(a) Outline of Argument (Submissions) filed 27 March 2026;

(b) Outline of Case filed 27 March 2026;

(c) Further Amended Initiation Application filed 23 March 2026;

(d) affidavit of Ms Van filed 23 March 2026;

(e) affidavit of Ms M (Family Report) filed 26 February 2026;

(f) Financial Statement filed 7 October 2025; and

(g) affidavit of Mr N filed 7 October 2025.

By way of his Outline of Case filed 26 March 2026, the husband relies upon:
(a) Financial Statement filed 23 March 2026;

(b) affidavit of Ms O filed 23 March 2026;

(c) affidavit of Mr Aixin filed 23 March 2026;

(d) affidavit of Ms M (Family Report) filed 26 February 2026;

(e) affidavit of Ms P filed 28 January 2026;

(f) Notice of Child Abuse, Family Violence or Risk filed 28 November 2025; and

(g) Response to Initiating Application filed 28 November 2025.

PROPOSALS

The wife seeks orders that:
(a) requiring the husband to make financial disclosure, including in relation to the Aixin Group;

(b) spousal maintenance in the sum of $61,435.55 per month; and

(c) litigation funding as a $250,000 lump sum or, in the alternative, a dollar-for-dollar order.

The husband opposes the orders sought by the wife. He further seeks orders that a single expert forensic accountant be appointed in relation to the wife’s expenditure from joint Westpac accounts, a single expert valuer be appointed in relation to entities associated with K Pty Ltd and the wife’s luxury bag and jewellery collection.

CONSIDERATION

Disclosure

Section 71B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) provides that each party to a proceeding relating to financial matters has a duty to give full and frank disclosure in a timely manner. Subsection (8) of that section notes that a party’s duty to disclose is a duty to disclose information known to the party and documents that are or have been in the possession or under the control of the party. Rule 6.06 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules (Cth) (“the Rules”), provides relevantly:
(1) Subrules (2) to (8) do not apply to a party to a financial or property proceeding who is not a party to the marriage or de facto relationship to which the application relates, except to the extent that the party’s financial circumstances are relevant to the issues in dispute.

(2) A party to a financial or property proceeding must make full and frank disclosure of the party’s financial circumstances, including the following:

(a) the party’s earnings, including income that is paid or assigned to another party, person or legal entity;
(b) any vested or contingent interest in property;
(c) any vested or contingent interest in property owned by a legal entity that is fully or partially owned or controlled by a party;

(d) any income earned by a legal entity fully or partially owned or controlled by a party, including income that is paid or assigned to any other party, person or legal entity;

(e) the party’s other financial resources;

(f) any trust:
(i) of which the party is the appointor or trustee; or

(ii) of which the party, the party’s child, spouse or de facto spouse is an eligible beneficiary as to capital or income; or

(iii) of which a corporation is an eligible beneficiary as to capital or income if the party, or the party’s child, spouse or de facto spouse is a shareholder or director of the corporation; or

(iv) over which the party has any direct or indirect power or control; or

(v) of which the party has the direct or indirect power to remove or appoint a trustee; or

(vi) of which the party has the power (whether subject to the concurrence of another person or not) to amend the terms; or

(vii) of which the party has the power to disapprove a proposed amendment of the terms or the appointment or removal of a trustee; or

(viii) over which a corporation has a power referred to in any of subparagraphs (iv) to (vii), if the party, the party’s child, spouse or de facto spouse is a director or shareholder of the corporation;

(g) any disposal of property (whether by sale, transfer, assignment or gift) made by the party, a legal entity referred to in paragraph (c), a corporation or a trust referred to in paragraph (f) that may affect, defeat or deplete a claim:
(i) in the 12 months immediately before the separation of the parties; or

(ii) since the final separation of the parties;

(h) liabilities and contingent liabilities.
The wife seeks disclosure of financial documents held by the different corporate entities comprising the Aixin group. The group is concerned with building developments and the volume of material sought to be disclosed over the period nominated (30 June 2019 to 30 June 2025) is likely to be voluminous.
On 6 March 2026, the wife filed various subpoenas in relation to the Aixin Group. The husband and the paternal grandmother filed Notices of Objections on behalf of a number of entities which form part of the Aixin Group. The Court made orders by consent on 30 March 2026 for written submissions to be filed and the issue of the objections were listed for hearing on 15 June 2026. At the hearing on 30 March 2026, counsel appeared for the objecting party.
I will not make orders in relation to the disclosure of documents by the entities referred to in the wife’s orders sought until argument is heard in full in relation to the objections, with the objectors being given the right to be heard. To do so otherwise would be procedurally unfair, particularly in light of the fact that the parties have agreed to defer consideration of those issues to 15 June 2026. A decision now in relation to the balance of the companies in the group may pre-empt the decision in relation to the objections.
In relation to the documents referred to in paragraph 5(e) of the wife’s application, being documents relating to C Pty Ltd and associated trusts, the husband gives evidence at paragraphs 53-54 of his affidavit that while he is listed on the company records as sole director of the trustee company and appointor and beneficiary of the trust, he has no legal or beneficial interest in that group. He goes on to state that he is in effect a front for a foreign investor and not the source of funds.
Those propositions may or may not be established at trial, however, the wife is entitled to test that evidence and the disclosure sought may be relevant to that exercise. I will make orders for the disclosure of documents by the husband in relation those entities as sought by the wife.

Spousal Maintenance

Section 72 of the Act provides that a party to a marriage is liable to maintain the other party, to the extent that the first-mentioned party is reasonable able to do so, if, and only if, that other party is unable to support themselves adequately whether:
(a) by reason of having the care of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years; or

(b) by reason of age or physical or mental incapacity for appropriate gainful employment; or

(c) for any other adequate reason;

having regard to any relevant matter referred to in subsection 75(2).
Section 75 of the Act provides that the matters to be taken into account when the Court exercises such jurisdiction are, amongst other things:
(a) the effect of family violence to which one party has subjected or exposed the other party;

(b) age and state of health of each of the parties;

(c) income, property and financial resources of each of the parties and the physical and mental capacity of each of them for appropriate gainful employment;

(d) the extent to which either party has the care of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years, including the need of either party to provide appropriate housing for such a child;

(e) commitments of each of the parties that are necessary to enable the party to support:

(i) himself or herself; and

(ii) a child or another person that the party has a duty to maintain; and

(f) the terms of any order made or proposed to be made under section 79 in relation to the property of the parties; and

(g) any other fact or circumstance which, in the opinion of the Court, the justice of the case requires to be taken into account.

The wife makes a claim that she requires $61,435.55 per month by way of spousal maintenance. In support of her application, the wife relies on, inter alia, her Financial Statement filed 7 October 2025. She says at paragraph 52 of her affidavit, that the figures in part N of the Financial Statement reflect the standard of living pre-separation. She says that the shortfall between her income (stated to be $1,370 per week in her Financial Statement) in relation to her expenditure is $61,435.55 per month. In her Financial Statement, the wife affirms that her average weekly expenses are:
(a) food expenses of $1,800 for herself and $200 for the child;

(b) the house repairs on a rental property where the rent is paid by the paternal grandmother at $200 per week;

(c) medical, dental and optical expenses of $300 which does not include health insurance premiums in circumstances where there is no mention of medical issues in her affidavit;

(d) entertainment/hobbies in the sum of $500 for herself and $200 for the child;

(e) gifts of $600, including an allowance of $100 for the child who is aged six years;

(f) holidays in the sum of $3,500;

(g) clothing and shoes in the sum of $2,000, said to be $1,850 for the wife and $150 for the child; and

(h) housekeeper expenses of $1,518 which is in addition to cleaning expenses of $100.

When these figures were raised with senior counsel for the wife, it was submitted that the figures represent the weekly costs associated with the lifestyle of high wealth people and should not be judged against ordinary community standards.
I regard the claims of the wife to require reasonable spousal maintenance, which includes such things as an allowance of $1,800 per week for her food, to be entirely fatuous.
Any submissions that the wife’s expenditure is justified by reason of the parties’ spending during the marriage with reference to the “family budget” is difficult to sustain where, as the husband submits, that budget included school fees, expenses for two properties, cars, international trips and a housekeeper and nannies. The child attends school and the husband meets his school fees.
In any case, it is clear that the parties’ previous lifestyle when married did involve a distinct element of extravagance. I do not find it appropriate, having regard to the circumstances, including the factors set out at s 75(2) of the Act, for orders to be made for the maintenance sought by the wife for the continuation of such a lavish lifestyle: see Wilson & Wilson [1989] FamCA 34; (1989) FLC 92-033, where their Honours held at 77,453:
... A standard of living that in all the circumstances is reasonable for the party claiming maintenance is not necessarily the same standard as that enjoyed by the party who is ordered to pay maintenance. ... Similarly, the standard of living that in all the circumstances is reasonable for the wife in this case, is not necessarily the same standard as that enjoyed during cohabitation. ...
It was suggested that if I did not regard the claims as reasonable, I should substitute some other figure which I did find to be reasonable. It was put that this would simply be the exercise of balancing claims and the Court making a determination of what was reasonable which was submitted as something the Court had to do on a regular basis.
I do not intend embarking on that exercise as it is incumbent on a party moving for orders to file evidence which might support the orders sought, or, in other words, the applicant must prove her case. I do not accept the evidence that the wife, for example, spending $1,800 per week on herself for food is reasonable. Similarly, I do not accept the expenditure of $2,000 per week on clothing as reasonable by whatever standards it is judged. It is not appropriate for the Court to have to embark on some other estimation of what is reasonable in the absence of credible evidence.
Further, the evidence is such that I do not accept that the wife lacks the capacity to support herself adequately or lacks financial resources. The wife is 33 years old, educated and healthy. The wife is employed operates a business which is described as providing financial services for ultra-high net worth individuals and for “sophisticated and professional investors only”. On the basis of the husband’s evidence, the wife has retained the benefit of $700,000 that she withdrew and retains from the parties’ $800,000 “baby bonus” upon the birth of the child in 2019. If the business she conducts is not currently profitable, there is no apparent reason why the wife cannot obtain alternative employment in the financial services industry given that she has the capacity to manage a business providing those services to sophisticated and/or professional investors.
There are deposits in the wife's bank accounts of $151,300 and debit card refunds of $187,450 that she received between September 2022 and July 2024. There are significant payments from her bank accounts in the sum of $55,158.22 to Q Pty Ltd, a foreign exchange company, on 8 May 2024 and 8 October 2024. Further, the sum of $70,000 was paid to a company called R Pty Ltd and $100,000 to S Pty Ltd on 2 September 2024.
On 14 May 2025, she withdrew $294,302.94 from T Pty Ltd into her own accounts. On 28 April 2025 until 1 May 2025, she transferred $800,000 out of her bank account to a development company whom the husband alleges is owned by her partner called U Pty Ltd.
The husband's evidence discloses that she told the husband that she raised over $20,000,000 for investments through her business in about December 2024, although she now describes the business as not being profitable. The husband's affidavit further discloses that the bank accounts for the business showed that it receives $11,000 per month from V Pty Ltd and another $11,000 per month from W Pty Ltd. both of which are entities said by the husband to be owned by her partner. The business spent $1,700 on “eating out” on 17 February 2026 and $2,000 on “eating out” on 23 February 2026.
The husband's income is recorded on his tax return for the financial year ending 2024 as $558,818. For the financial year ending 2025, the wife's taxable income was recorded as $108,740 or $2,091 per week, which comprised of income from the Aixin Foundation of $64,259, rent from her property in the sum of $30,453 less interest and $65,000 from J Property Fund. In total, the return shows tax payable of $25,012.19, leaving a net income of $83,727.81 or $1,610 per week. The wife also receives $500 per week in child support from the husband and a further $1,000 each week in spousal maintenance by reason of the orders made 16 December 2025. In the wife's hands each week, therefore, is the sum of $2,610 plus $500 in child support. Again, the husband pays the child's school fees and the paternal grandmother is responsible for her rent until August 2026.
I do not find that the wife has established that she has a need for spousal maintenance. There is no liability to pay spousal maintenance unless the need of the applicant is first established. If the applicant cannot establish an inability (which will not be inferred) to support themselves, then an order for maintenance cannot and will not be made (Rowan and Rowan [1977] FamCA 100; (1977) FLC 90-310; Stacy and Stacy (1977) FLC 90-324 and Parsons & Parsons [2023] FedCFamC1F 382 at [32]- [38]).

Litigation Funding

Section 114UB(2) of the Act provides that:
(2) If, in proceedings under this Act, the court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the court may, subject to subsection (3) of this section, sections 114UC and 114UD and the applicable Rules of Court, make any order for costs and security for costs that the court considers just, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise.

(3) In considering what costs order (if any) should be made, the court must have regard to the following:

(a) the financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings;

(b) whether any party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings and, if so, the terms of the grant of the assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure under subsection 71B(1), 90RI(1) or 90YJA(1);

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer;

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.

In Naumov & Gesissler (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 612, Campton J stated:
There is a long line of authority that provides power to the Court to make a litigation funding order, including on a dollar-for-dollar basis, pursuant to s 114UB of the Act (Charisteas & Charisteas [2022] FedCFamC1A 160; (2022) FLC 94-109 at [75]‑[77]).
Before an order as to costs can be made, the Court must be satisfied that the order is, in the circumstances, just (s 114UB(2) of the Act). Until that point, there is no power to make an order departing from the general rule that each party pays their own costs (s 114UB(1)). Once enlivened, the discretion to make an order as to costs is governed by the considerations contained in s 114UB(3) of the Act. There is no authority that contends that more than one of the factors in s 114UB(3) need be present. Indeed, any one factor may be determinative.
In Salvage & Fosse [2020] FamCAFC 144; (2020) FLC 93-966, the Full Court identified that litigation funding orders have a long history and are made to alleviate the obvious unfairness of a party with control of the assets being able to marshal them to pay lawyers, leaving the other party to attempt to pursue the proceedings without being able to resort to property that might subsequently be transferred to them. That said, each application must be looked at according to its own particular facts and circumstances. The principles relevant to the making of a litigation funding order were identified by the Full Court in Zschokke and Zschokke [1996] FamCA 79; (1996) FLC 92-693, being:
(a) A position of relative strength on the part of the respondent;
(b) A capacity on the part of the respondent to meet his or her own legal costs; and

(c) An inability on the part of the applicant to meet his or her legal costs.
In Atkins & Hunt and Ors [2018] FamCA 14; (2018) 57 Fam LR 128, Watts J noted:
The making of a dollar for dollar order is a discretionary order that is usually made only as an order of last resort. Examples of circumstances which may attract the exercise of the discretion include:
46.1. Where:
46.1.1. The party who apparently controls significant financial purse strings pleads impecuniosity; and

46.1.2. The financially disadvantaged party cannot point to any particular fund or asset that might be available to help assist that party fund their litigation; but

46.1.3. The financially advantaged party seems to be able to fund their litigation through personal exertion income or structures that they directly or indirectly control (see for example, Zadenev & Zadenev [2014] FamCA 693 and Ryder & Bonham);
46.2. Where a financially advantaged party is a minority shareholder in a company and/or a discretionary beneficiary in a trust and directors or trustees seem more than willing to declare dividends or make distributions to fund litigation for the financially advantaged party against the disadvantaged party;

46.3. Where a financially advantaged party has relatives or associates who are prepared to fund the litigation against the financially disadvantaged party in circumstances where the financially disadvantaged party does not have the same support from third parties (Hurford & Hurford [2016] FamCA 328).
While it is the case the wife has borrowed funds for legal expenses and may have a capacity to borrow further funds, she is in a financially disadvantaged position as against the husband by reason of his access to funds from relatives and the financial resources they are said to control (the question of which remains to be determined). I acknowledge the husband’s submissions that the wife has not established that she cannot borrow further funds, however, it is apparent that the husband has had access to funds from his family and their associated companies in order to fund his legal costs. I therefore find it appropriate in the circumstances that there be a dollar-for-dollar costs order.
Given the quantum of the costs that each party estimates that they will spend if this matter proceeds to a full hearing, and the uncertainty in relation to the quantum of the claims made by the wife, I will order that this order remain in place until 1 September 2026 or until the conclusion of a mediation, whichever is sooner.

CONCLUSION

I will make orders to give effect to my findings above.
I will also order that the parties exchange written offers of settlement in relation to the property matters in accordance with r 4.11 of the Rules, with the husband to make an offer within 14 days of the date of this order and the wife to make a response by way or acceptance or counteroffer within 28 days.
The parties must commence any mediation by 1 September 2026. I note that the parties previously consented to orders to attend mediation by orders made on 16 December 2025 but that this mediation has not taken place.
